composes.note: The Playbook layer (timeline, decisions, shopping, quantities, purchase/cooking/setup/cleanup schedules, vendor options) lives in dinner-party.playbook.json. This EOS file adds the Readiness, Experience, Guest-Management, and Host-Mindset layers that sit on top of it.

_comment: eventState fields referenced by predicates (deterministic). Some exist today (guests, vendors, date, budget); the new ones (menu.locked, dietary.collected, recipes.new, seating.capacity, help.hired) are the signals to start capturing. Engine degrades gracefully: an unknown field reads as 'unmet/unknown', never a false 'ready'.

communications.message: Invite with date, time, full address, what-to-bring (or 'nothing — just yourself'), and a one-line dietary/allergy ask. Set a soft RSVP-by date.
Collect allergies + restrictions IN the invite — never after the menu is set.
Arrival window ('come at 7, we'll eat around 7:45'), parking instructions, and whether to bring anything. Removes the early-arrival-while-cooking trap.
If BYOB or 'bring a bottle', say so explicitly so the alcohol plan and the guests align.

seatingPlan.rule: Assign seats (place cards) for 8+; seat complementary energies together; split known conflicts; put the shy guest next to the connector, not the wall.

conflictHandling.trigger: known guest conflict on the list
conflictHandling.guidance: Seat them apart, brief no one, and have a co-host or yourself anchor the table that needs steadying.

mental.label: Menu is fully finalized (incl. a vegetarian main + allergy swaps)
mental.fix: Lock all three courses; write the dish list down. Everything downstream depends on this.

mental.label: Guest count is stable (RSVPs in, no churn)
mental.fix: Chase the maybes; treat the locked number as final for shopping.

mental.label: Budget is realistic for the guest count
mental.fix: Sanity-check against ~$30–120/guest; trade the protein down a tier before cutting the wine.

mental.label: You know your oven/stove + fridge can actually run this menu
mental.fix: Map each dish to a burner/oven slot + temp; if two dishes need different oven temps at the same time, change one.

mental.label: You've read every recipe start-to-finish
mental.fix: Read for hidden time-sinks ('marinate overnight', 'rest 1h') BEFORE you build the cook schedule.

physical.label: Enough chairs for everyone
physical.fix: Count chairs vs RSVPs; borrow or rent folding chairs now, not the day-of.

physical.label: Enough matching place settings (plates, glasses, flatware)
physical.fix: Need ~2 plates + 2.5 glasses + 1 flatware set per guest (with breakage spares); borrow/rent the gap.

physical.label: Serving platters AND serving utensils for every dish
physical.fix: One serving spoon/tongs per dish — the single most-forgotten item. Lay them out the afternoon-of.

physical.label: Fridge space for ingredients + chilling drinks
physical.fix: Clear a shelf; use a cooler + ice for drinks so the fridge holds food.

physical.label: Ice on the day-of list (~1.5 lb/guest)
physical.fix: Buy ice the morning-of; pre-chill whites 3h ahead.

physical.label: Parking / arrival logistics are clear
physical.fix: Tell guests where to park; one sentence prevents ten texts.

emotional.label: First-time-host nerves
emotional.reassurance: Nerves mean you care. Your guests came for your company, not a restaurant. A relaxed host beats a flawless plate every time.
emotional.redirectTo: Pick a make-ahead-heavy menu so you can actually be at the table.

emotional.label: Menu-ambition pressure
emotional.reassurance: Wanting to impress is normal — but trying 2+ new recipes the same night is the #1 dinner-party regret.
emotional.redirectTo: Keep at most ONE new dish; make it a course you can finish early. Cook the rest from things you've made before.

emotional.label: Tablescape / comparison spiral
emotional.reassurance: The internet's tablescapes are styled for cameras, not dinners. Candles + flowers + clean glasses is genuinely enough.
emotional.redirectTo: Stop polishing decor; the bigger lever is whatever readiness gap is still open.

emotional.label: Decision fatigue
emotional.reassurance: Too many small choices is draining and normal. Defaulting is a strategy, not a failure.
emotional.redirectTo: Take the recommended default on the low-stakes calls (one cocktail, open seating under 8) and save energy for the menu.

emotional.label: Fear of running out of food/drink
emotional.reassurance: Almost every host over-buys. Plan ~10% over on proteins and ½ bottle of wine per drinker — that's plenty, and leftovers are a gift.
emotional.redirectTo: Buy the planned quantities; resist the panic-buy.

social.label: Dietary/allergy collected from ALL guests
social.fix: One unflagged severe allergy is the only thing here that can hurt someone. Collect with the invite; confirm at RSVP close; always plate one safe vegetarian main.

social.label: Arrival expectations communicated
social.fix: 'Come at 7, dinner ~7:45' stops the early-arrival-while-you're-cooking scramble.

social.label: Alcohol expectations are clear (host-provides vs BYOB)
social.fix: Say it in the invite; always have a great zero-proof option for non-drinkers/DDs/pregnant guests.

social.label: Known guest conflicts are managed (seating)
social.fix: Seat apart; don't announce it; anchor the table yourself.

experience.what: Cooking a never-before-tried recipe live, with guests watching
experience.why: Untested recipes hide time-sinks and failure points; discovering them at 7pm with 8 guests is the classic disaster.
experience.fix: Test it once beforehand, or move the new dish to a make-ahead course you can redo if it flops.

experience.what: Two or more courses that must be cooked à-la-minute
experience.why: You end up trapped at the stove instead of with your guests — the most common host regret.
experience.fix: Design so ≥2 of 3 courses are make-ahead/reheat; you should PLATE, not COOK, once guests arrive.

experience.why: Warm drinks, no way to chill the just-arrived rosé. Almost always forgotten.
experience.fix: Add ~1.5 lb ice/guest to the day-of run.

experience.why: Plated, beautiful food and nothing to serve it with — a day-of scramble.
experience.fix: Lay out one serving utensil per dish during table setup.

experience.why: There is no graceful recovery at 7pm for a missing chair.
experience.fix: Reconcile capacity vs RSVPs at T-7d; borrow/rent before the count over-fills.

experience.what: Two dishes need the oven at different temperatures at the same time
experience.why: A real constraint hosts discover mid-cook with no fix.
experience.fix: Map dishes to oven/burner slots in advance; stagger or swap a dish.

experience.why: Wilted greens, off fish, sad herbs.
experience.fix: Buy fresh 24–36h out, after headcount locks; pantry/alcohol can go earlier.

experience.what: Hosting + cooking + bartending + cleaning solo for 8+
experience.why: No single person enjoys their own party doing all four jobs.
experience.fix: Recruit a co-host for drinks, or spend $100–180 on a post-party cleaner — the highest-ROI sanity move.

experience.what: Starting table setup an hour before
experience.why: You're plating decor while guests ring the bell.
experience.fix: Set the table in the afternoon; light candles 15 min before.

mindset.philosophy: The goal is not a longer to-do list — it's a calmer, more confident host. Each week, surface the ONE thing that matters most, normalize the feeling, and redirect energy away from low-stakes busywork toward the real lever.
rules: Rank open concerns by severity × time-pressure; coach the single highest, not the whole list.
If the host is spending time on a low-severity item (decor, extra cocktails) while a higher-severity readiness check is unmet, name the swap explicitly.
Always pair a redirect with reassurance — tell them what's normal at this stage.
Critical social/safety gaps (dietary/allergy) outrank everything, at every stage.

phases.title: Decide
phases.focus: Lock the menu and collect dietary restrictions.
phases.mental: The menu is the spine — shopping, quantities, and your cook schedule all flow from it. Decide it and write it down.
phases.emotional: It's normal to feel ambitious. Choosing a menu where 2 of 3 courses are make-ahead isn't a cop-out — it's the pro move that lets you be at the table.
phases.social: Ask for allergies and restrictions NOW, in the invite. Never after the menu is set.
phases.physical: Glance at chair + place-setting counts so you know if there's a gap to fill.
phases.reassurance: You don't need the final shopping list yet. One good decision this week (the menu) is the whole job.

phases.title: Plan
phases.focus: Confirm headcount, build the shopping plan, decide on any help.
phases.mental: With the menu locked, this is mechanical: list what to buy and when, and reserve any rentals/help.
phases.emotional: Decision fatigue is real this stretch. Take the recommended defaults on small calls (one signature cocktail, open seating under 8) and save your energy.
phases.social: Send arrival-time + parking logistics so no one shows up 30 minutes early while you're cooking.
phases.physical: Secure the chair/serveware gap now — borrowing the day-of is how parties go sideways.
phases.reassurance: Almost every host over-buys; plan ~10% over on proteins and ½ bottle of wine per drinker and stop there.
phases.redirectFrom: Adding a third cocktail option or a more elaborate dessert.

phases.title: Acquire & Prep
phases.focus: Pantry/alcohol shop, then fresh, then make-ahead cooking.
phases.mental: Buy non-perishables + alcohol first; buy fresh 24–36h out after headcount locks.
phases.emotional: This is the busy stretch, and that's expected. The make-ahead cooking you do tonight is exactly what lets you relax tomorrow.
phases.social: Confirm the final count and re-check the one allergy you flagged.
phases.physical: Do full mise en place — chop, marinate, portion. A staged kitchen is a calm host.
phases.reassurance: Feeling behind the night before is normal; the braise in the fridge means you're actually ahead.

phases.title: Execute
phases.focus: Set up in the afternoon; plate (don't cook) when guests arrive; stage cleanup.
phases.mental: Reheat make-ahead, finish the one live dish, dress the salad last. Plate — don't start cooking — once the bell rings.
phases.emotional: Whatever isn't perfect, your guests won't notice. They came for you. A host who sits down beats a host who hides in the kitchen.
phases.social: Greet, pour a drink, point to the bathroom. Warmth is the whole job now.
phases.physical: Empty the dishwasher and stage a bus tub before anyone arrives; clear courses into it — don't wash mid-party.
phases.reassurance: Something small will go a little wrong at every dinner party ever thrown. That's not failure — that's a dinner party.
phases.redirectFrom: Last-minute new dishes or re-plating for looks.

flagshipExample.situation: Host has spent two evenings comparing centerpieces; 14 guests invited; dietary not yet collected.
flagshipExample.coaching: Centerpieces are the fun part — but the real risk right now is that you haven't heard back on dietary restrictions from 14 guests, and your menu locks this week. Send the allergy ask today; the table can wait. (This is normal — decor feels productive because it's pleasant; the engine's job is to point you at the lever that actually matters.)

contingencies.trigger: A dish burns / breaks / doesn't set
contingencies.plan: Keep a rescue course in the pantry — good dried pasta + a jar of quality sauce + parmesan is a respectable plate in 15 minutes. Don't announce the failure; just serve the pivot.
You already set 1–2 buffer place settings and bought ~10% extra; stretch the meal with more bread, salad, and sides. A gracious host never lets the guest feel the math.
Pour another round and serve the most complex course family-style instead of plated, or cut it entirely. Guests remember the mood, never the missing course.
Open with bread + cheese, stretch with a fast pasta or a big salad, and slow the pacing. Calm reframes 'short' as 'relaxed'.

contingencies.trigger: Oven or a key appliance fails
contingencies.plan: Pivot to stovetop / grill / no-cook (charcuterie + salad + pasta). Know in advance which dish is your appliance-independent fallback.

contingencies.trigger: A guest reveals a restriction you didn't know about
contingencies.plan: The plated vegetarian main + a side of plain protein/grain is your universal safe plate. This is exactly why one safe veg main is non-negotiable.
The ½-bottle-per-drinker rule should prevent this; if it happens, the nearest store run or the BYOB ask covers it. Never let it run fully dry — switch to a punch/spritz to stretch.
Club soda + the spare place setting you kept aside. Reset and move on without a fuss — your calm sets the table's mood.

plannerLens.purpose: What a senior planner is actually thinking about — the meta-level the checklist never shows. The engine surfaces this so the user borrows a pro's judgment, not just their tasks.
successDeterminants: The food is SAFE (allergies) and ON TIME — everything else is taste.
The HOST is present and relaxed — a host trapped in the kitchen is the failure mode, not an imperfect plate.
Guests feel TAKEN CARE OF — greeted, a drink in hand, told where the bathroom is.
alwaysBuffers: +1–2 place settings for surprise +1s and breakage
+20 minutes of slack in the cook schedule

thinkingByPhase.proWeighs: Can this menu be cooked by ONE person while also hosting? If not, simplify or get help — before falling in love with the dishes.
Where are the single points of failure (one oven, one host, one protein source)? Add a buffer or a backup to each.
What can move earlier? Every make-ahead task done the night before is risk pulled out of the live window.
Read the room over the plan. If guests are happy and slow, let the timeline drift. The schedule serves the party, not the reverse.

plannerLens.theOneThing: A relaxed, present host with safe, on-time food will throw a 'successful' dinner party every time — even if three small things go wrong. Optimize for that, not for perfection.
plannerLens.nextActionContract: At any moment the engine answers 'what would an experienced planner do next?' by taking the single highest-severity OPEN item across Solve (timing), Readiness (gaps), Experience (likely failure), and Mindset (focus) — time-weighted — and stating it with the WHY and the reassurance. Never a flat list; always the one next move.
